note: Lehi's prophetic calling parallels Jeremiah's commission as a prophet to the nations.
Lehi's throne-room vision parallels Isaiah's vision of the Lord on His throne.
Nephi's faith to go and do echoes Abraham's faith to obey without knowing the destination.
The principle that one man should die to save a nation parallels Caiaphas's unwitting prophecy about Christ.
Lehi discovers his descent from Joseph who was sold into Egypt.
The Tree of Life imagery connects to John's apocalyptic vision of the tree bearing twelve fruits.
Nephi's vision of the virgin bearing the Son of God is fulfilled in the annunciation to Mary.
The loss of plain and precious truths is elaborated further in Nephi's later writings.
The great and abominable church parallels the whore of Babylon in John's Revelation.
Nephi recounts the parting of the Red Sea to chasten his brothers' lack of faith.
Alma later quotes the same prophet Zenos on prayer and the Atonement.
Nephi quotes Isaiah 48 nearly verbatim from the brass plates.
Nephi quotes Isaiah 49, the prophecy of the Lord's servant and Israel's gathering.
Nephi's millennial prophecy connects to Christ's own words to the Nephites about the last days.
Alma's discourse on justice and mercy expands the Fall/Atonement framework Lehi introduces here.
Adam and Eve's declaration of joy in the Fall parallels Lehi's teaching that men exist to have joy.
The prophecy of a choice seer is fulfilled in Joseph Smith's calling as described in D&C.
Amulek's teaching on the infinite and eternal sacrifice echoes Jacob's discourse on the infinite atonement.
Joseph Smith provides the inspired interpretation of the stem of Jesse and the root of Jesse.
The relationship between grace and works is a central doctrinal connection between Nephi and Paul.
The encounter with the learned man (Martin Harris and Charles Anthon) fulfills this prophecy.
Nephi's earlier vision of the great and abominable church connects to these latter-day deceptions.
Christ personally teaches the same doctrine of Christ that Nephi outlines here.
Alma later echoes the principle that the word of God has more power than the sword.
The sign of Christ's birth parallels the nativity account in Luke.
The destructions in the Americas coincide with the crucifixion events in Jerusalem.
Christ invites people to feel His wounds, paralleling His appearance to Thomas.
Christ delivers the Sermon at the Temple, closely paralleling the Sermon on the Mount.
Continuation of the Sermon paralleling Matthew 6 on prayer and fasting.
Conclusion of the Sermon paralleling Matthew 7 on judgment and foundations.
Christ identifies the Nephites as the 'other sheep' He spoke of in John.
The Abrahamic covenant that all families of the earth shall be blessed.
Christ quotes Isaiah 54 on the expansion and peace of Zion.
Christ commands the Nephites to record Malachi 3 on tithing and the Lord's messenger.
The promise of Elijah is fulfilled in Moroni's visit to Joseph Smith and the Kirtland Temple.
The naming of Christ's Church is reiterated in the modern-day revelation.
The desire of the Three Nephites parallels the tradition about John the Beloved.
The Nephite Zion society of 'all things common' parallels the early Christian church in Jerusalem.
The elimination of all -ites fulfills Nephi's teaching that God invites all to come unto Him regardless of distinction.
Ammaron instructs Mormon to retrieve the records, directly connecting to the opening of the book of Mormon.
The discovery of the people of Zarahemla and the Mulekite population is further detailed in Mosiah.
Coriantumr's discovery connects the Jaredite civilization to the Nephite record.
Amulek's detailed teaching on prayer echoes Enos's model of persistent, heartfelt supplication.
The witness received after the trial of faith parallels Moroni's teaching on faith preceding evidence.
The promise of receiving truth through sincere prayer connects to Moroni's closing invitation.
The worth of souls and the joy of bringing them to Christ parallels Enos's expanding circle of concern.
Nephi's original statement about making the small plates for a wise purpose is fulfilled in Mormon's discovery.
The Lord's wise purpose for the small plates is revealed when the 116 lost pages are replaced by this record.
Mormon's bridge narrative connects directly to the opening of Mosiah and King Benjamin's reign.
King Benjamin's sermon on caring for the poor expands Jacob's teaching to think of brethren as yourselves.
The law of consecration echoes Jacob's teaching to seek the kingdom of God before riches.
Paul's teaching on Israel's blindness parallels Jacob's concept of looking beyond the mark.
Isaiah's vineyard parable uses similar imagery of God's care for Israel and their failure to produce good fruit.
Paul's olive tree metaphor of grafting Gentiles into Israel parallels Zenos's allegory directly.
Korihor's confrontation with Alma mirrors the Sherem-Jacob encounter as a pattern of anti-Christ opposition.
The Tower of Babel account parallels the Jaredite departure narrative.
The barge construction echoes Noah's ark as a vessel of divine deliverance.
The pre-mortal Christ appearing in bodily form connects to the doctrine that God has a tangible body.
The Jaredite ocean crossing parallels the Lehite voyage in pattern and purpose.
Secret combinations are traced back to Cain's covenant with Satan.
The Gadianton robbers followed the same pattern of secret oaths from Cain.
The faith chapter in Ether parallels and complements the faith chapter in Hebrews.
The promise that weakness becomes strength through grace connects to the promise of the Holy Ghost's constant companionship.
The New Jerusalem prophecy parallels John's vision in Revelation.
The final Jaredite battle at Ramah/Cumorah foreshadows the final Nephite battle at the same location.
The 'inasmuch as ye keep the commandments ye shall prosper' promise is reiterated.
Building on the rock of the Redeemer connects to the baptismal covenant in 2 Nephi.
Nephi cites Moses lifting up the serpent as a type of Christ, as does Jesus to Nicodemus.
The sealing power given to Nephi parallels the keys given to Peter.
Mormon's commentary on man's frailty echoes the Psalmist's 'He knoweth our frame.'
Samuel's rebuke about rejecting prophets mirrors Jesus's lament over Jerusalem.
The new star at Christ's birth parallels the star followed by the Wise Men.
Ammaron's charge to Mormon directly follows the record-keeping chain from 4 Nephi.
The sorrowing of the damned vs godly sorrow parallels Paul's teaching on true repentance.
Mormon's refusal to lead connects to the principle that vengeance belongs to the Lord.
Mormon's lament at Cumorah echoes the final Jaredite destruction at the same location.
Mormon's testimony of the Bible and Book of Mormon working together fulfills Nephi's vision.
Moroni's description of latter-day pride echoes Nephi's earlier prophecies of the last days.
The unchangeable nature of God parallels the Hebrews declaration of Christ's consistency.
Moroni records the words Christ spoke about conferring the Holy Ghost.
The sacramental prayer is repeated verbatim in modern revelation.
The prayer on the wine is repeated verbatim in D&C.
Mormon's discourse on charity closely parallels Paul's chapter on love.
The salvation of little children is confirmed in Joseph Smith's celestial vision.
Moroni's list of spiritual gifts parallels Paul's enumeration.
The modern revelation on spiritual gifts expands on Moroni's and Paul's teachings.
Benjamin's teaching on serving God through service to others parallels Christ's sheep and goats parable.
The angel's description of Christ's birth parallels Luke's nativity account.
The role of a seer as described by Ammon is confirmed in modern revelation.
Abinadi quotes the Ten Commandments directly from the brass plates.
Abinadi quotes Isaiah 53 on the suffering servant.
Abinadi's Christology connects to the modern revelation on the nature of Christ.
The baptismal covenant at the Waters of Mormon parallels the modern baptismal requirements.
The Lord easing burdens connects to Alma's later prayer for strength under affliction.
Alma the Younger's conversion account is retold in greater detail in Alma 36.
The reign of judges begins in Alma 1 with the immediate challenges Alma faces.
Alma refers to his own conversion and spiritual rebirth.
Prophecy of Christ bearing our griefs and sorrows.
Universal nature of the resurrection.
The account of Melchizedek as a high priest.
The definition of faith parallels Paul's formulation.
The Liahona's original description and discovery.
The necessity of opposition and the law in the plan of redemption.
The call of Abraham to leave his country.
Those who are Christ's are Abraham's seed and heirs.
Intelligence was not created or made.
Parallel creation account.
Joseph Smith's prayer fulfills James's invitation to ask of God.
The recorded words of John the Baptist when conferring the Aaronic Priesthood.
Prophecy of Elijah's return quoted by Moroni with slight variations.
Joseph Smith—Matthew is a more correct translation and expansion of Matthew 24.
The Lord gives a similar revelation concerning the signs of His coming to Joseph Smith in 1831.
References the abomination of desolation spoken of by Daniel the prophet.
Both passages describe Christ as the means of salvation and the purpose of God's work.
Moses 2 is the revealed restoration of the Genesis creation account.
Both texts describe the premortal council and Satan's rebellion.
Links the Fall of Adam to the necessary conditions for human existence and joy.
Describes a society living in a Zion-like state of unity and peace.
Both passages define the nature of the Godhead.
Nephi outlines the same first principles and ordinances.
Expands on the gathering of Israel and the New Jerusalem.
Both texts describe Josiah's Passover celebration.
Opening verses are nearly identical regarding Cyrus's decree.
Both provide lists of Jewish families returning from captivity.
Introduces Ezra and his genealogy.
Both describe the public reading of the Law.
Antiochus setting up the abomination of desolation fulfills Daniel's prophecy.
Mattathias's zeal compared to Phinehas.
Trust in God to deliver the many by the few echoes Jonathan's faith.
The NT mentions the Feast of Dedication established here.
Every man under his vine and fig tree mirrors the prophetic vision of peace.
The imagery of a hen gathering her chickens is used by both the Lord in Esdras and Jesus in the New Testament.
The eagle vision in Esdras parallels Daniel's vision of the fourth beast with many horns.
The Man from the sea destroying the wicked parallels Christ's coming in the book of Revelation.
Both passages discuss the scattering and eventual gathering of the lost tribes of Israel.
The concept of sealed or reserved scriptures for the wise parallels instructions given in the Restoration.
Both texts emphasize the literal nature of the resurrection of the body.
Both books record the cleansing of the temple by Judas Maccabeus.
The practice of offering for the dead relates to the LDS doctrine of work for the dead.
The vision of deceased prophets looking after the living parallels the LDS view of the spirit world.
Refers to the light and joy that came to the Jews after their deliverance.
Both texts emphasize that God does whatever He pleases because of His supreme power.
Esther's recognition of her weakness and need for God's grace parallels the themes of humility in the Book of Mormon.
The king's heart being turned by God is a literal example of the proverb.
The formal record of the king's decree allowing the Jews to defend themselves.
Both texts use near-identical language to confess that righteousness belongs to God while shame belongs to Israel.
The horrors mentioned here are the fulfillment of the curses of the law for breaking the covenant.
Christian tradition often links the wisdom conversing with men to the incarnation of Jesus Christ.
Both describe the gathering of the children of Israel from the four quarters of the earth.
Both passages describe Jeremiah sending a letter to the captives in Babylon.
This serves as a second account of Daniel being cast into a den of lions for his faith.
Both passages contrast the worship of man-made idols with the worship of the living God.
Daniel's prayer echoes the theme that the Lord does not forsake those who seek Him.
Both texts emphasize the turning to God through fasting and sackcloth during times of crisis.
Achior's statement about prosperity contingent on righteousness matches the promise given to the Nephites.
Judith references the zeal of Simeon and Levi in avenging the defilement of their sister Dinah.
The beheading of Holofernes by Judith is a literary and thematic parallel to Jael killing Sisera.
Both Judith and David use the enemy's own sword to decapitate their respective oppressors.
This book is an addition to the narrative of the three Hebrews in the fiery furnace found in Daniel.
Both passages acknowledge God's justice in punishing Israel for their disobedience.
The 'Song of the Three Holy Children' parallels the structure and themes of Psalm 148, calling all creation to praise God.
This prayer is the traditional text of the prayer mentioned in the historical account of Manasseh's repentance while in Babylonian captivity.
Both passages emphasize the Lord's willingness to forgive those who confess their sins with a humble heart.
Manasseh's 'bowing the knee of the heart' parallels Alma the Younger's internal cry for mercy during his intense repentance.
Shared wisdom theme regarding the Fear of the Lord as the beginning of knowledge.
Refinement theme (gold tried in the fire) as a reason for trials.
Core doctrine of moral agency and the choice between life and death.
Logos/Wisdom theology regarding the pre-mortal role of the Word in creation.
Mandate to forgive others to be forgiven by God.
God's special attention to the prayer and status of the poor.
Descriptions of the beauty and liturgical function of the Aaronic priestly garments.
Susanna's refusal to sin against God mirrors Joseph's response to Potiphar's wife.
The execution of the elders follows the Mosaic law that a false witness receives the punishment they intended for the accused.
Strong parallels in the doctrinal emphasis on almsgiving and caring for the poor as a spiritual necessity.
Parallels regarding the sanctity and purpose of the marriage relationship.
Common imagery of angels presenting the prayers of the saints before the throne of God.
Descriptions of the New Jerusalem built with gold and precious stones.
Striking parallel to the mockery of Jesus Christ on the cross regarding being the Son of God.
The peaceful state of the righteous after death.
The Armor of God imagery used to describe divine attributes turned to military purpose.
Description of Christ (Wisdom) as the brightness of God's glory and the express image of His person.
The concept of the mortal body as a tabernacle or burden that weighs down the soul.
Refers to the brazen serpent as a sign of salvation where healing comes from looking toward God.
Refers to the gift of Manna as angels' food.
The Natural Man — both passages teach about the natural man's inability to receive spiritual things.
Glory of the faithful — unimaginable things God has prepared for those who love Him.
Body as temple — the Spirit of God dwells in the Saints.
Institution of the Sacrament — parallel accounts of the Lord's Supper.
Creation of woman — neither man nor woman is complete without the other in the Lord.
Baptism for the dead — the primary biblical evidence for proxy ordinances.
Degrees of Glory — celestial, terrestrial, and telestial kingdoms.
Universal resurrection — as in Adam all die, so in Christ shall all be made alive.
Both texts emphasize the power of the Atonement to cleanse those who return to the Lord.
LDS scripture provides a direct parallel to Christ's role as our Advocate with the Father.
Mormon echoes John's teaching that we must have pure love to be like Christ when He appears.
The Joseph Smith Translation clarifies that God is seen by those who have faith and the Spirit.
Confirms the unity of the Father, Son, and Holy Ghost as one Godhead.
Peter quotes the Old Testament command to be holy.
Quotation regarding the transience of flesh and endurance of the Word.
The prophecy of the rejected stone becoming the head of the corner.
Reference to Israel being a kingdom of priests.
LDS revelation expanding on Christ's ministry in the spirit world.
The principle that love covers a multitude of sins.
Peter cites the teaching that God gives grace to the humble.
